title: Let 'start' optionally return the process ID of the container's entry point
body:
### Feature request description

Currently, `podman start <CONTAINER>` returns the name or ID of the container in standard output.  Would it be possible to optionally return the process ID of the container's entry point?  This could optionally replace the container name or ID, or be in addition to it in JSON or Go template form.

When a Toolbx container is started for the first time, Toolbx needs to synchronize `podman start` spawning the entry point, the entry point finishing the run-time initialization of the container, and presenting the user with a `podman exec` session running an interactive command line shell.  This is done by the entry point signaling the end of initialization by creating a stamp file based on its PID, and `podman exec` is invoked only after this file is in place.

So, currently, Toolbox invokes `podman start` to spawn the entry point and `podman inspect` to get its PID.

This sequence of steps happens as part of `toolbox enter` and `toolbox run`, which is how one actually uses a Toolbx container.  Therefore, optimizing this code path by reducing the number of commands that are forked and exec:ed has a positive impact on the user experience.  I am hoping that we don't have to do the `podman inspect`, by making `podman start` give us everything that we need.

There's also the minor issue of a race between the `start` and `inspect`, but that's not the reason for this feature request.

### Suggest potential solution

One possibility is to add a `--format` option to `podman start`:
```ShellSession
$ podman start --format json <CONTAINER>
[
  {
    "Id": "012345abcdef",
    "State": {
      "Pid": 123
    }
  }
]
```

This has the advantage of being extensible.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

The alternative is to run `podman start` followed by `podman inspect`, which is what we are doing at the moment.

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body: Would adding `--pid-file` to `podman start` be sufficient?
@mheon and I talked about this and that was also my thought.  I would prefer to not introduce another `--format` generic option when something specific like this would suffice?

@baude @mheon I am not very tied to `--format` or any specific implementation, as long as it's machine readable.

Will `podman start --pid-file` work like `crun exec --pid-file`?  One passes a path to a file and Podman will write the PID to it?  That should work, yes!
Yes, you give us a path, we write PID to a file at that path.

> Yes, you give us a path, we write PID to a file at that path.

Fantastic!  That sounds good to me for Toolbx.

> When a Toolbx container is started for the first time, Toolbx needs to synchronize podman start spawning the entry point, the entry point finishing the run-time initialization of the container, and presenting the user with a podman exec session running an interactive command line shell. This is done by the entry point signaling the end of initialization by creating a stamp file based on its PID, and podman exec is invoked only after this file is in place.

Would it be better to switch to using the sdnotify mechanism? If you expose an sdnotify socket to podman and the create the container with --sdnotify container (which is the default) the socket is leaked into the container. Then your container process connects to the socket once it is ready and sends the ready message. Then the host process reads that message and knows the process is ready. Sure a bit more complicated code wise but it should be much more robust. There is no need to handle/ read the pid at all (which might be invalid/another process already if the container exits) and there would be no need to poll until the file is created.

---

While I am not strictly against a --pid-file option I have a couple of concerns with it, a podman start can take 1 to N containers so how would that option work if multiple containers are started. Second, while it is easy to expose pid information in the local podman client this would have to work on the rmeote client as well which then means we also would need to modify the REST API to send the pid back as well.

Easiest solution for `--pid-file` with 2+ containers is error - having it support more than one container seems unnecessary for most use cases.

For the remote API: From the current manpage for `podman run --pid-file`: `(This option is not available with the remote Podman client, including Mac and Windows (excluding WSL2) machines)` - so I think doing the same here is appropriate. Getting the PID on a remote host that can't do anything with the PID seems unnecessary in general.

On a bit of further investigation, I noticed that `--pidfile` during `podman create` is persistent - every subsequent start of the container will also create a PID file at the specified location. Is that sufficient for Toolbx - you can specify a PID file at create time, and every subsequent start can assume the same location?

> Would it be better to switch to using the sdnotify mechanism? If you expose an sdnotify socket to podman and the create the container with --sdnotify container (which is the default) the socket is leaked into the container. Then your container process connects to the socket once it is ready and sends the ready message. Then the host process reads that message and knows the process is ready. Sure a bit more complicated code wise but it should be much more robust. There is no need to handle/ read the pid at all (which might be invalid/another process already if the container exits)

Is a  systemd service unit mandatory for using the `sd_notify(3)` mechanism?  Or, can I fake it by creating a randomly named local UNIX socket and setting its path to the `NOTIFY_SOCKET` environment variable?  I am asking because creating a systemd service unit for each container might be a bit awkward.

I tried to fake this with:
```ShellSession
$ ncat --listen --udp --unixsock --verbose --verbose /tmp/test.sock
```

... and:
```ShellSession
$ export NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/test.sock
$ podman run -it --rm --sdnotify container registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:42 /bin/sh
sh-5.2#
```

However `ncat(1)` exits with exit code 2 and `connect: Invalid argument` as soon as the container starts.

Will it work if the socket doesn't exist when the container is created, but only before the container is started?  I suppose, I will need a different socket for each container?

> and there would be no need to poll until the file is created.

I am using `inotify(7)` through [fsnotify.Watcher](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/fsnotify/fsnotify) instead of literally polling the file in a busy loop, unless for some reason I get `ENOMEM` or `ENOSPC` from `inotify(7)`.  I suppose if I use a socket instead of file system events, then I would change my Go channel to read from a different source.

> On a bit of further investigation, I noticed that `--pidfile` during `podman create` is persistent - every subsequent start of the container will also create a PID file at the specified location. Is that sufficient for Toolbx - you can specify a PID file at create time, and every subsequent start can assume the same location?

Interesting.  I didn't expect `podman create --pidfile` to exist.

I am worried that if I use a persistent well-known location, then someone can intercept the communication and insert something else into the file.  That's why I was hoping to supply a randomly named transient temporary file.

Good thing is that I can adapt the `--pidfile` code for `create` and `run` to put together a pull request for `start`.

> > Would it be better to switch to using the sdnotify mechanism? If you expose an sdnotify socket to podman and the create the container with --sdnotify container (which is the default) the socket is leaked into the container. Then your container process connects to the socket once it is ready and sends the ready message. Then the host process reads that message and knows the process is ready. Sure a bit more complicated code wise but it should be much more robust. There is no need to handle/ read the pid at all (which might be invalid/another process already if the container exits)
> 
> Is a systemd service unit mandatory for using the `sd_notify(3)` mechanism? Or, can I fake it by creating a randomly named local UNIX socket and setting its path to the `NOTIFY_SOCKET` environment variable? I am asking because creating a systemd service unit for each container might be a bit awkward.

Yeah my suggestion was you fake it. We do that as well for some cases, https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/2b647bf3f87d2a75e1a4c118c4cc04672bdfbdce/pkg/systemd/notifyproxy/notifyproxy.go#L70

> 
> I tried to fake this with:
> 
> $ ncat --listen --udp --unixsock --verbose --verbose /tmp/test.sock
> 
> ... and:
> 
> $ export NOTIFY_SOCKET=/tmp/test.sock
> $ podman run -it --rm --sdnotify container registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:42 /bin/sh
> sh-5.2#
> 
> However `ncat(1)` exits with exit code 2 and `connect: Invalid argument` as soon as the container starts.
> 

Not sure about ncat specifically but we test with socat, i.e. https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/test/system/260-sdnotify.bats

> Will it work if the socket doesn't exist when the container is created, but only before the container is started? I suppose, I will need a different socket for each container?

It should always just use what NOTIFY_SOCKET is set to so on each start you could set a different path via the env. If the env is not set we just skip the notify code path. 

> 
> > and there would be no need to poll until the file is created.
> 
> I am using `inotify(7)` through [fsnotify.Watcher](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/fsnotify/fsnotify) instead of literally polling the file in a busy loop, unless for some reason I get `ENOMEM` or `ENOSPC` from `inotify(7). I suppose if I use a socket instead of file system events, then I would change my Go channel to read from a different source.

Fair enough, I guess overall sdnotify might not offer any advantage for your use case then. The advantage of sd notify in systemd is that you can send many different messages, but if you only care about ready then the socket handling code is likely more complex then the fsnotify code

> Yeah my suggestion was you fake it. We do that as well for some cases, 

I see, ok.  Thanks for the explanation.

After sleeping over it, I feel inclined to stick to the `podman start --pidfile ...` approach over `podman create --sdnotify ...`.  Rubber duck explanation below.  :)

From a high level view, there is some value in keeping the Toolbx container's entry point only loosely coupled with the rest of what `toolbox enter` does, because it makes it easier to manually call `podman start ...` outside the usual sequence of things that happen when `toolbox enter` is invoked.  This is useful when trying to reproduce a problem with only `podman(1)` commands, or when trying to trigger races and process synchronization problems by changing the timing of events, or when something is really broken with the entry point because the entry point of Toolbx containers is a weird monster.

If we use a `sd_notify(3)` socket to have the entry point communicate with the host, then it becomes convoluted when asking a bug reporter to manually run `podman start` separately from `toolbox enter`.  A socket needs to be present that the host would be listening to; otherwise, there would be no notification and the host won't proceed to `podman exec ...`.  I suppose it would be easier if we could use a FIFO or named pipe, because it's easier to create one from a CLI shell.

The current approach with an initialization stamp file based on the entry point's process ID avoids this problem, because both sides can come to an agreement without an explicit handshake.

I will concede that the possibility that the entry point's process ID might quietly become invalid has bothered me at times.  I suppose the entry point could unexpectedly crash or something could maliciously kill it and/or try to replace it with something else.

It seems like this gets mitigated by careful error handling?  If `toolbox(1)` mistakenly thinks that a container is running, even though it's not, then it will either time out waiting for the initialization stamp file to be created or it will get an error from trying to invoke `podman exec` against a stopped container.

A similar problem can also happen with the `sd_notify(3)` socket, no?  If the entry point unexpectedly disappears, then either the notification will go missing or something malicious might send it.

> The advantage of sd notify in systemd is that you can send many
> different messages, but if you only care about ready then the socket
> handling code is likely more complex then the fsnotify code

I did have a need to share a FIFO or a socket between the host and the container's entry point when I was looking for a way to forward the entry point's logs to `toolbox --verbose enter` to be shown directly on the CLI.  Finally, I settled for running `podman logs` on the host in a goroutine and [io.Pipe()](https://pkg.go.dev/io), instead of an explicitly shared FIFO or socket, for the same reasons as above.
